coredata.dc:description: Decorating electron-accepting materials on carbon nitride (C3N4) is a promising strategy to construct heterostructure catalysts for improved photocatalytic abilities. In this study, B-doped carbon-dots (B-C-dots) decorated C3N4 (C3N4/B-C-dots) catalysts were fabricated through the surface deposition. The benefits from integration of B-C-dots and C3N4 are four folds: (i) increasing surface area; (ii) improving visible light absorption; (iii) promoting the transfer of photoinduced carriers; and (iv) reducing the recombination of photoinduced carriers. The optimum photocatalytic activity of B-C-dots/C3N4 for Rhodamine B (Rh B) (or tetracycline hydrochloride (TC)) degradation was about 7.21 (6.56) and 4.80 (4.35) times higher than that of C3N4 and C-dots/C3N4, respectively, exhibiting both remarkable stability and repeatability. Moreover, enhanced photocatalytic activity of C3N4/B-C-dots could also be attributed to the type-II heterojunction formed between C3N4 and B-C-dots caused by B doping.

TEM images of (a) C3N4; (b) C3N4/C-dots; (c) C3N4/B-C-dots.
Photoluminescence emission spectra under the excitation of 375 nm.
Transient photocurrent response (a) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (b).
Influence of B doping on the physical adsorption (a) and photocatalytic ability (b) of C-dots for Rh B.

Adsorption capacity of as-prepared photocatalysts for Rh B and TC after the establishment of absorption-desorption equilibrium (40 min in dark) (a); photocatalytic ability of the catalysts based on the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B and TC under visible light (b)/(c); corresponding kinetic simulation curve (d)/(e) and cycle runs of C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 for Rh B removal (f).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Decorating electron-accepting materials on carbon nitride (C3N4) is a promising strategy to construct heterostructure catalysts for improved photocatalytic abilities. In this study, B-doped carbon-dots (B-C-dots) decorated C3N4 (C3N4/B-C-dots) catalysts were fabricated through the surface deposition. The benefits from integration of B-C-dots and C3N4 are four folds: (i) increasing surface area; (ii) improving visible light absorption; (iii) promoting the transfer of photoinduced carriers; and (iv) reducing the recombination of photoinduced carriers. The optimum photocatalytic activity of B-C-dots/C3N4 for Rhodamine B (Rh B) (or tetracycline hydrochloride (TC)) degradation was about 7.21 (6.56) and 4.80 (4.35) times higher than that of C3N4 and C-dots/C3N4, respectively, exhibiting both remarkable stability and repeatability. Moreover, enhanced photocatalytic activity of C3N4/B-C-dots could also be attributed to the type-II heterojunction formed between C3N4 and B-C-dots caused by B doping.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis has been recognized as a promising strategy to solve energy crisis [1–3] and environmental issues [4–15] by exploiting the inexhaustible and sustainable solar energy [16–22]. Synthesis of highly efficient and eco-friendly photocatalysts plays a vital role in the utilization of photocatalytic technology. Carbon nitride (C3N4) has been studied as an appealing metal-free photocatalyst due to its good visible light response, chemical stability, low cost and non-toxicity [23–26]. However, the practical application of bulk C3N4 is restricted by its low separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers and poor photocatalytic capability [27,28]. To solve those issues, various approaches have been used such as morphology modulation [29–31], chemical doping [32–34], surface integration of electron-accepting materials [35–37] and construction of heterojunction [38–40]. Amongst the myriad, integration of electron-accepting materials on C3N4 is generally adopted to improve the separation and transport of photoinduced carriers. Moreover, nanoporous carbons have been employed as electron acceptors to combine with C3N4 for the construction of heterojunction photocatalysts with an improved photocatalytic activity [41–43].

Carbon nanodots (C-dots) as a zero-dimensional carbon have been successfully applied in conjunction with semiconductor materials, e.g., WO3, TiO2, Ag3PO4 and Fe2O3, to promote their photocatalytic abilities [33,37,41,42,44–46]. For example, Liu et al. [47] reported the design and fabrication of metal-free C-dots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites with excellent photocatalytic properties. The quantum efficiencies of as-prepared C-dots/g-C3N4 reached 16, 6.29 and 4.42% for the wavelength (λ) of 420 ± 20, 580 ± 15 and 600 ± 10 nm, respectively. These C-dots/g-C3N4 photocatalysts are made of low-cost, earth-abundant, environmental-friendly materials and also exhibit a high stability. For example, Gao et al. [35] investigated the interaction between g-C3N4 and trigonal/hexagonal shaped C-dots by density functional theory (DFT) calculations and found that the type II van der Waals heterojunction formed between g-C3N4 and C-dots led to a significant reduction of band gap. The C-dots decorated g-C3N4 promoted the separation of photoinduced carriers and enhanced the visible light response. The band alignment of C-dots and g-C3N4 confirmed that C-dots could act as a spectral sensitizer in the hybrid catalyst for water splitting. For example, Ma et al. [48] characterized the interfacial electron coupling of C-dots/g-C3N4 composites based on hybrid functional calculations including long-range dispersion correction. The results indicated that the band gap of C-dots/g-C3N4 could be manipulated by changing the lateral size of C-dots, and the C24H12/g-C3N4 exhibited an ideal band gap of 1.92 eV for harvesting the solar light. The strong electron depletion above C-dots sheet and the electron accumulation below g-C3N4 monolayer induced by charge redistribution at interface potentially ameliorated the separation of H2O oxidation and reduction reactions. Feng et al. [30] described the influence of surface functional groups (such as OH, CHO and COOH) of C-dots on the excited states of C-dots/g-C3N4 catalyst by the first-principles of many-body Green’s function theory. They found that the formation of charge-transfer excited state at the interfaces caused both high separation rate and low recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. The surface functional groups of C-dots played a vital role in determining the charge transfer direction, active sites for the reduction-oxidation reaction of water, and the reaction process (four-electron process or two-electron/two-electron process). Ong et al. [46] unraveled the charge carrier dynamics in the protonated g-C3N4/C-dots heterojunction catalysts (p-C3N4/C-dots) with an enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction through a combined experimental and first-principles of DFT study. The significantly improved photoactivity of p-C3N4/C-dots can be ascribed to the synergistic interaction between p-C3N4 and C-dots, promoted effective migration of photoinduced electrons from p-C3N4 to C-dots and restricted charge recombination. The results calculated by DFT also suggest that the efficient shuttling of electrons from the conduction band of p-C3N4 to C-dots retarded the recombination of photoinduced carriers. Although significant progress has been accomplished in the design and fabrication of high efficiency C-dots/g-C3N4 hybrid photocatalysts, the incorporation of C-dots into g-C3N4 to fabricate novel and efficient photocatalysts still remains largely unexplored.

In this work, B-doped carbon nanodots (B-C-dots) were decorated on the g-C3N4 surface to fabricate C3N4/B-C-dots heterostructures. The resulting C3N4/B-C-dots hybrid materials were explored for visible light responsive photocatalytic ability. Compared with C3N4 and C3N4/C-dots, the photoactivity of C3N4/B-C-dots was markedly enhanced with the increased doping amount of B in the C-dots. The C3N4/B-C-dots catalysts exhibited excellent durability and recyclability. However, an excessive doping amount of B element reduced the band gap energy, and thus deteriorated the photocatalytic performance of C3N4/B-C-dots. A novel viewpoint about the photocatalytic mechanism of C3N4/B-C-dots has been put forward in this study. The B doping treatment can make the transition of B-C-dots from semimetal to semiconductor, which results in the formation of type-II heterojuncation between C3N4 and B-C-dots, thus facilitates the efficient separation of electrons and holes.

The chopped carbon fibers (T300) were supplied by Guangwei Co. Ltd (China). Nitric acid, potassium hydroxide, boric acid, Rhodamine B, tetracycline hydrochloride, phenol, and melamine were purchased by Aladdin Co. Ltd (China). All chemicals, reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

Firstly, 2 g chopped carbon fibers were immersed in 200 mL nitric acid (68 wt%) at 85 °C for two weeks. Potassium hydroxide powders were added in the acid solution slowly for acid-base neutralization until pH = 7. Subsequently, the neutral solution was dialysed to remove salt. The mass concentration of finished obtained aqueous solution of C-dots was approximately 8 g·L−1.

Certain quantity boric acid and 50 mL aqueous solution of C-dots were mixed in a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor until boric acid was completely dissolved. Then, the obtained solution was sealed and heated at 180 °C for 14 h. After natural cooling, the solution containing B-C-dots was centrifuged, washed with ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 9 h. According to the mass ratio of C-dots and boric acid (1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4 and 0.2), the as-prepared B-C-dots were labeled as B-C-dots-1.0, B-C-dots-0.8, B-C-dots-0.6, B-C-dots-0.4 and B-C-dots-0.2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C3N4, C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: C3N4 was prepared by thermal pyrolysis of melamine at 550 °C for 4 h (heating rate: 2 °C/min). The obtained yellow powders were grinded for subsequent use. 200 mg C3N4 and 10 mg C-dots or B-C-dots were dispersed in 100 mL ethanol for ultrasonic treatment of 4 h. Then, the mixture was centrifuged and dried. The as-prepared samples were noted as C3N4/C-dots, C3N4/B-C-dots-1.0, C3N4/B-C-dots-0.8, C3N4/B-C-dots-0.6, C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 and C3N4/B-C- dots-0.2, respectively. For comparison, the used pure C3N4 was also ultrasonic treated under the same conditions.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a D8 advanced X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. Surface morphology was characterized by FESEM (Hitachi S-4800, Japan) and HRTEM (Hitachi JEM 2100, Japan). Surface groups were analyzed using FTIR spectra (Bruker VERTEX 80/80v, Germany). The specific surface areas and porosity were obtained by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method on a Micromeritics ASAP2020 HD88 specific area under a relative pressure of 10−6 MPa at 77 K. Optical properties were evaluated by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2550, Japan) and PL spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4500, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα source. The total organic carbon (TOC) content of the samples was analyzed by a TOC-VCPH analyzer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photo-electrochemical measurements were performed on an electrode-chemical workstation (CHI 660E, China) using a conventional three-electrode cell with 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as electrolyte. An Ag/AgCl and a Pt plate were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared as follows. Briefly, 20 mg catalyst powders were dispersed in the mixed solution of ethanol (10 mL) and nafion solution (100 µL) by ultrasound treatment for 40 min. The obtained slurry (300 µL) was spotted onto a 2 cm × 2 cm fluorine-doped tin-oxide (FTO) glass substrate. The working electrode was dried using an infrared lamp. A Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300, Education Au-light Co., China) with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the light source. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of the active radicals were obtained on a spectrometer (JES-FA200, Bruker) using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a probe molecule.

The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples was evaluated by the decolorization of Rhodamine B (Rh B), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and phenol aqueous solution using a Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300, Education Au-light Co., China) with a 420 nm cutoff filter as the light source. Briefly, 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed into 100 mL Rh B (or TC) aqueous solution (10 mg/L), and continuously stirred in a completely dark environment for 40 min to ensure the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and Rh B or TC. A 7 mL mixed solution for Rh B or TC concentration analysis was collected and centrifuged to remove the solid catalyst at given time intervals. The concentration change of Rh B or TC was monitored by the optical absorption at 552 or 357 nm during the photoreaction process.

The stability of the photocatalysts was assessed by five recycled photodegradation experiments of Rh B solution. After each photodegradation test, the photocatalysts were collected according to a typical procedure: rinsing with ultrapure water, centrifugation, and desiccation at 353 K for 7 h. Before the next photodegradation reaction, the loss of recollected sample was supplemented by adding a certain quantity of unused photocatalyst.

In free radicals trapping experiments, three different scavengers were used for quenching active oxidation species (namely, p-benzoquinone (BQ) (1 mM, Aladdin, 99%) for
O2 −, isopropanol (IPA) (0.5 mM, Aladdin) for
OH and ammonium oxalate (AO) (0.05 mM, Aladdin, 99%) for holes). The volume ratio of each scavenger to Rh B solution used in the experiment was 1:100.

The B-C-dots possess irregular spherical shape, the distribution is uniform and no aggregation of nanoparticles is observed (Fig. S1(a), Supporting Information). The particle sizes of B-C-dots range from 5 to 25 nm (Fig. S1(c)), and the average diameter has been calculated to be 13.5 nm. The observed clear lattice fringe with a lattice spacing of 0.228 nm in B-C-dots corresponds to the (1 0 0) plane of graphitic carbon [49]. Fig. S2 compares the morphology of C3N4 before and after decoration of C-dots. Generally, C3N4 polymer molecule with two-dimensional structure can be synthesized through the thermal polymerization of nitrogen-rich precursors. Macro-scale C3N4 is formed by the stacking of two-dimensional C3N4 polymer molecules. The generation and release of NH3 during the thermal pyrolysis process can enlarge the space between C3N4 polymer molecules, distort the planar structure of molecules, thus form the lamellar structure of C3N4. Nevertheless, the C-dots can hardly be seen from the SEM images of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots due to the shelter effect derived from the stacking of C3N4 sheets. The surface element composition was also investigated by XPS analysis (Fig. 1 (a)/(b) and Table S1). The surface of C3N4 and C3N4/C-dots merely comprises of C, N and O element. The introduction of C-dots containing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups makes the amount of C and O element increase from 38.09 to 55.43 wt% and from 2.33 to 4.17 wt%, respectively. After the decoration of B-C-dots, the characteristic peak (at 189.1 eV) of B element, i.e., 2.29 wt%, appears in C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4. The corresponding amount of C, O and N element changes to 53.81, 3.93 and 39.97 wt%, respectively. Meanwhile, the deconvoluted B1s spectrum confirms that the B atoms exist in three distinct chemical environments in our samples (Fig. 1(b)). The intense peak at about 189.1 eV is attributed to the in-plane BC3 type (i.e., B replacing C in the hexagonal lattice) [35]. The low signal located at 191.2 eV is ascribed to the presence of borinic ester (C2BO) and boronic acid (CBO2) moieties [28]. Fig. 2 displays the graphite-like lamellar structure of C3N4, which demonstrates the successful decoration of C-dots and B-C-dots on the C3N4 surface (as illustrated in red dashed circle of TEM images).

The specific surface area and porosity were assessed by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. C3N4, C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots all possess the isotherms of type IV with a hysteresis loop at a higher relative pressure according to the IUPAC classification (Fig. S3(a)), indicating the existence of mesopores and macropores. Fig. S3(b) presents the pore size which falls mainly within the range of 3 ~ 13 nm. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface area (S BET) of C3N4, C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots was determined to be ~24.29, ~36.15 and ~42.84 m2/g, respectively. The enlarged BET surface area of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots was ascribed to the more mesoporous structure supplied by C-dots or B-C-dots. The average pore size was calculated to be 8.34, 13.47 and 14.52 nm for C3N4, C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots, respectively, confirming the formation of mesoporous materials. It is believed that a larger S BET can supply more abundant reaction activity sites and enhances the adsorption of degradation molecules on the surface of catalysts [50]. C3N4/B-C-dots were thus anticipated to act as more promising photocatalytic materials compared to C3N4 and C3N4/C-dots.

The crystal structure of samples was investigated by the XRD spectra (Fig. S4). A broad diffraction peak at around 25° in the XRD pattern of C-dots suggests that the C-dots consisting of disordered graphitic-like carbon have a low crystallinity [35]. The stronger diffraction peak at about 27.6° of pristine C3N4 corresponds to the interlayer stacking of aromatic segments with a distance of 0.32 nm called as (0 0 2) plane of the stacking of conjugated aromatic system [32]. Noticeably, the XRD patterns of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots nearly coincide with that of pristine C3N4 due to the possible low crystallinity of C-dots.

The surface groups of as-prepared catalysts were investigated by FTIR spectra (Fig. S5). The B-C-dots were well water-dispersible due to the presence of carboxylic groups at the B-C-dots surface. The broad absorption peak at about 3412 cm−1 is attributed to the OH stretching vibration [51], whereas the absorption peaks centered at 1738 and 1352 cm−1 are ascribed to the CO stretching vibration derived from carbonyl and carboxylic groups, respectively [52]. The characteristic peak at around 1243 cm−1 is identified as the infrared vibration of CO bond [53]. The observed two absorption peaks at 3214 and 1046 cm−1 from BO and BC strength modes, respectively, also confirm the successful doping of B element into the C-dots matrix [28]. For C3N4, the absorption peaks between 3350 and 3000 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH [54]. The typical peaks in the range of 1750–1180 cm−1 belong to the CN and CH stretching vibration. The absorption peak at about 810 cm−1 arises from the triazine units of C3N4 [55]. The C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots display similar FTIR spectra to C3N4 due to the low content of C-dots or B-C-dots. The above analysis of FTIR spectra indicates that the chemical structures of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots are in good accordance with that of the C3N4.

The light harvesting ability as a crucial parameter for photocatalytic activity was investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). The fundamental absorption edge of C3N4 (at ~460 nm) (Fig. 1(c)) was consistent with previous reports [27]. After the decoration of C-dots, the light harvesting capability of C3N4/C-dots was improved remarkably in the range of 450–800 nm, confirming that the C-dots improved the light absorption capacity. Compared to C3N4/C-dots, C3N4/B-C-dots exhibited a better light absorption ability at a longer wavelength (460–600 nm). The absorption edge of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots (located at ~466 and ~471 nm, respectively) exhibited a red-shift in comparison to pure C3N4. Moreover, the energy band edge (E g) was calculated by the following equation: ahv = A(hv − E g) n
/2 [56]. The E g values can be estimated from the intercept of tangents to a plot of (ahv)1/2 versus hv (n = 4 for the indirect transition) from the UV–vis spectra [25]. As displayed in Fig. 1(d), the bandgap of C3N4 was calculated to be 2.45 eV. The introduction of C-dots and B-C-dots impels the bandgap of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots to be reduced to 2.41 and 2.38 eV, respectively, implying the formation of subband energy levels between C-dots and C3N4. This assumption is further verified by the photoluminescence (PL) spectra. In addition, the valence band (VB) of photocatalysts was further examined by the valence-band XPS spectra (Fig. 3 ). The VB values of C3N4, C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 were determined to be 1.89, 1.75 and 1.72 eV, respectively. Subsequently, the conduction band (CB) was calculated by the following equation: E CB = E VB − E g [57]. The E CB position of C3N4, C3N4/C-dots and C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 was confirmed to be −0.56, −0.66 and −0.66 eV, respectively.

The separation and migration efficiencies of the photoinduced carriers for the as-prepared catalysts were evaluated by PL spectra. Fig. 4 shows a broad luminescence peak located at about 462 nm. The PL emission derives from the recombination of photoinduced carriers. The pristine C3N4 was expected to possess a rapid recombination rate of charges duo to its strong PL emission peak. The C3N4/C-dots exhibited lower PL intensities than C3N4 owing to the fact that C-dots attracted the photogenerated electrons and inhibited the direct recombination with the holes. Compared with the results for C3N4 and C3N4/C-dots, the relative intensity of C3N4/B-C-dots was much lower due to the structural imperfection of C-dot caused by the B doping trapped photogenerated electrons and the impeded recombination of photoinduced carriers. The above analysis proved that the separation efficiency of the photoinduced carriers could be enhanced by surface decoration of B-C-dots/C3N4.

Here, transient photocurrent generation and electrochemical impedance (EIS) spectroscopy were used to further investigate the transfer and separation behaviors of photoinduced charges. The renewable photocurrent density during the three “on-off” irradiation cycles exhibits good stability of catalysts (Fig. 5 (a)). The photocurrent density of C3N4/C-dots was more than 0.081 μA/cm2, whereas C3N4 showed less than 0.021 μA/cm2. The C3N4/B-C-dots exhibited the highest photocurrent intensity (more than 0.098 μA/cm2), which was almost 1.21 and 4.67 times higher than that of C3N4/C-dots and C3N4, respectively, confirming the positive effect of B-C-dots on the separation and transfer efficiencies of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This is in good agreement with the weakest PL emission peak and the strongest photocurrent intensity. C3N4/B-C-dots had the smallest diameter of semicircular Nyquist plot (Fig. 5(b)), implying a higher separation of the photogenerated charges and higher transfer efficiencies following the decoration of B-C-dots.

The photocatalytic ability of the as-prepared catalysts was evaluated by the degradation of Rh B and TC aqueous solutions under the irradiation of visible light (λ > 420 nm) at room temperature. Before light irradiation, the mixed solution of catalysts and dye was stirred for 40 min in dark for the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The near-complete saturation at the solid/liquid interface before starting the photocatalytic reaction can weaken the influence of physical adsorption on the variation of Rh B or TC concentration during the photocatalytic reaction [58]. The influence of B doping on physical adsorption and photocatalytic activity of C-dots for Rh B is illustrated in Fig. 6 . The evaluation of physical adsorption in dark (4 h) showed that the adsorption-desorption equilibrium has been established within 30 min. The removal rate of Rh B caused by physical adsorption of C-dots and B-C-dots was 11.31 and 17.63%, respectively. The enhanced physical adsorption of B-C-dots could be ascribed to its enlarged surface area and increased pore volume caused by the B doping treatment [28]. After 180-min irradiation under visible light, the photocatalytic activity of C-dots was negligible (as shown in Fig. 6(b)). Surprisingly, the removal of Rh B from the solution with B-C-dots was 30.85%. Generally, heteroatom doping can effectively open a band gap in graphene, resulting in its transition from semimetal to semiconductor [59]. Therefore, B-C-dots exhibited a better photocatalytic ability compared to that of C-dots. The physical adsorption of C3N4 and C3N4/C-dots for Rh B (or TC) was about 9.75 (or 5.43) and 12.97 (8.55) %, respectively (Fig. 7 (a)). All the C3N4/B-C-dots exhibited a higher adsorption capacity in comparison to C3N4/C-dots, consistent with the results of S BET. The enhanced adsorption capacity of C3N4/B-C-dots was attributed to the larger surface area and greater pore space. Due to the photocatalytic reaction that occurred only on the surface of catalysts, the photocatalytic efficiency was closely related to the surface adsorption characteristics of catalysts. The improvement of dye adsorption should promote the rapid diffusion of contaminants molecules from the bulk solution to the surface of catalyst, thus achieving a faster photocatalytic degradation.

Fig. 7(b) displays the variation of Rh B concentration during photocatalytic reaction. The concentration of Rh B was almost unchanged in the absence of catalyst under irradiation. The removal of Rh B reached 51.35 and 66.26%, respectively, after adding C3N4 or C3N4/C-dots under visible light irradiation for 120 min. As for C3N4/B-C-dots, a significant increased removal of Rh B was observed under the identical conditions due to the positive effect of B-C-dots. Moreover, the increasing of B doping level led to an enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of C3N4/B-C-dots, but excess B doping resulted in a deterioration of photocatalytic activity. Among all the C3N4/B-C-dots samples, C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 exhibited the highest removal percentage of Rh B (about 97.96% within 100 min) and a physical adsorption capacity of Rh B (around 14.64%). Interestingly, similar phenomena and rule were observed in the degradation of TC (Fig. 7(c)). The removal percentage of TC reached 52.94 and 65.82% in the presence of C3N4 or C3N4/C-dots after visible light irradiation for 180 min. Relative to other samples, C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 possessed the highest removal percentage (about 98.73%) and a physical adsorption capacity for TC (around 11.83%). Meanwhile, the comparison of photocatalytic activity was made between C3N4/B-C-dots and the reported B-doped C3N4. The calculated photocatalytic degradation rate per unit weight catalyst for B-doped C3N4 of about 0.5 mg (Rh B)/(min·g) from literature was significantlylowerthan that of C3N4/B-C-dots (2 mg (Rh B)/(min·g)) [60]. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of C3N4/B-C-dots was superior to that of B-doped C3N4. Additionally, phenol was also used to evaluate the photocatalytic capability. As illustrated in Fig. S6, C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 possessed the highest photocatalytic activity among all samples. The removal percentage of phenol reached 43.15% after 180 min photocatalytic degradation.

Further detailed formation of the underlying photoreaction was obtained by the rate of photocatalytic oxidation calculated by Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model [61]:

where R, kr , θ, K and C are the reaction rate, the reaction rate constant, the surface coverage, and the adsorption coefficient of the reactant and the reactant concentration, respectively. Because C is in millimolar level and KC is negligible with respect to unity in this study, Eq. (1) can be simplified to the following pseudo first-order rate expression:

where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the reactant concentrations at times t = 0 and t, respectively and kapp is the apparent reaction rate constant.

The photocatalytic stability of catalyst is another important index for its practical applications. The cycling experiments of C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 were performed for Rh B photodegradation (Fig. 7(f)). C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 exhibited an inappreciable loss of photocatalytic ability after 5-cycle reaction process, revealing high stability of C3N4/B-C-dots. The FTIR and XRD spectra of C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 before and after five cycling are depicted in Fig. S7. No clear discrepancy was found in surface groups and phase structure of the recycled C3N4/B-C-dots in comparison with that of unirradiated one.

In addition, the degree of mineralization is very important for the degradation of pollutant since the intermediate product might possess a more virulent form. Therefore, the TOC removal efficiency during the photocatalytic degradation process was also investigated in our study. The TOC removal efficiency of Rh B and TC over C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 increased with increasing the irradiation time (Fig. S8). About 91.83% Rh B and 62.59% TC are removed and mineralized to CO2 and H2O under visible light irradiation for 180 min. The mineralization process indicates that the C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 has a very high mineralization ability. This strongly suggests that the C3N4/B-C-dots are an effective visible light responsive photocatalyst with a high durability.

The radical species play a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B aqueous solution. To investigate the primary active species, ammonium oxalate (AO), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) have been employed as the typical scavengers of h+,
O2 − and
OH, respectively [62]. The removal of Rh B was inhibited from 97.96% to 52.51% and 74.44% when BQ and AO were added, respectively (Fig. 8 (a)). The addition of IPA exhibited negligible inhibition on the photocatalytic performance, implying that
OH had a minimal impact on the degradation of Rh B.

Subsequently, electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to further investigate the effect of
O2 − and
OH radicals for the removal of organic pollutants. DMPO as a scavenger was dissolved in a methanol and water solution to detect the presence of
O2 − and
OH during reactions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8(b)/(c), no ESR signal was observed when the sample was exposed in the dark, whereas the signals were clearly detected under visible light irradiation. This indicates the importance of visible light for the generation of
O2 − and
OH. Six characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 — (Fig. 8(b)) were detected for C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 under visible light irradiation within 5 min, on account of the single electron reduction of O2 to
O2 −. Fig. 8(c) illustrates that the weak DMPO-
OH peaks for C3N4/B-C-dots-0.4 were tested after 5 min irradiation. To sum up, h+,
O2 − and
OH were generated during the photodegradation reaction, and h+ and
O2 − played a predominant role toward the degradation of organic pollutants.

The significantly improved photocatalytic activity of C3N4/B-C-dots could be attributed to the enlarged surface absorption, enhanced light-harvesting ability, and more effective charge separation and transfer. Based on the above results, a potential mechanism was proposed for the enhanced photocatalytic ability of C3N4/B-C-dots (Fig. 9 ). The B doping treatment results in the transition of B-C-dots from semimetal to semiconductor and the formation of doping valence [63]. The doping of a B atom will introduce a doping band above the valence band and lower Fermi energy below the center of the forbidden band, which is in favor of utilizing the visible light, the generation and separation of free electron-hole pairs. Gao et al. [24] calculated the valence band maximum (VBM, i.e., highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)) and conduction band minimum (CBM, i.e., lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO)) potential of trigonal and hexagonal shaped C-dots semiconductors by ab initio studies and found that the HOMO level of C-dots semiconductors is in the middle of bandgap of C3N4, while the LUMO of C-dots is above the CBM of C3N4. The type-II band alignment formed between C3N4 and C-dots could further enhance the visible-light response [35]. This observation was also confirmed by our study (Fig. S9). The C3N4/B-C-dots were fabricated by the type-II heterojunction between C3N4 and B-C-dots. The type-II heterojunction plays a critical role in the charge separation and photocatalytic performance of C3N4/B-C-dots. When the C3N4/B-C-dots catalyst was irradiated by visible light, free electrons- holes pairs were generated and separated on C3N4 (or B-C-dots). The electrons were photoexcited from the VB to the CB, thus leaving holes in the VB. Due to the function of type-II heterojuncation, the photogenerated electrons could be easily transferred from CB of B-C-dots to the CB of C3N4, and the photogenerated holes on VB of C3N4 were transferred to the VB of B-C-dots, which facilitated the efficient separation of electrons and holes, thus improved the photocatalytic performance.

In this study, B-C-dots were prepared and decorated on C3N4 surface through surface deposition method for the improved activity of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollution. The obtained C3N4/B-C-dots shows a high photocatalytic activity compared to pristine C3N4 and C-dots/C3N4. The experimental results indicate that the B-C-dots/C3N4 has the maximum specific surface area, the best visible light absorption capacity, and the fastest charge transfer among all the samples studied. Meanwhile, the cycling experiments also confirm the excellent photocatalytic stability of B-C-dots/C3N4. In the photocatalytic degradation process, h+ and
O2 − are the dominant active species for the oxidization and degradation of contaminants. A novel viewpoint about photocatalytic mechanism has been put forward in our study. The B doping treatment makes the transition of B-C-dots from semimetal to semiconductor, which results in the formation of type-II heterojuncation between C3N4 and B-C-dots, thus facilitates the efficient separation of electrons and holes. The strategy developed herein provides a novel avenue for developing high effective C3N4 based hybrid photocatalytic materials by decoration of quantum dots and for other potential application like sensors, water splitting, etc. [64-66].
